title: Scrambling in Joshua Tree
summary:
For our first destination of the new year, we decided to visit Joshua Tree. The forecast was sunny and (relatively) warm and we were both excited to do some climbing and scrambling. We arrived at the first come, first served Hidden Valley Campground and were able to grab the last available spot. Unfortunately, unless you arrive at the campground before noon, there is no way to pay for and secure your spot unless you drive to a park entrance. So, we left a folding chair and a note at the site to hold our spot and made the 20 minute drive back to the entrance. After squaring away our registration, we headed over to Cyclops Rock to get in a little bit of climbing before sunset. We climbed two pitches and got a feel for the style of climbing in Joshua Tree. To me, it felt very reminiscent of climbing on the coarse granite of my home crag at Lolo Pass, which made me feel at home there. The next morning, we sought out climbs that were within 100 feet of camp, as those are the only off-road areas that dogs are permitted in the park. We were able to find a handful of routes around camp that fit the bill, the highlight being a cool 10a with some unique movement called Pinched Rib. We spent the afternoon going on a little run down the road, playing chess at camp and planning for the next stops on our adventure. Our final day in the park, we headed to an area called Indian Cove, where many routes are accessible to crag dogs. We climbed a few easy crack pitches, then top roped a classic 5.11 called Monaco, which featured many consecutive moves on featureless slab. That evening, we camped at the dispersed camping outside of the park. The campground was a flat, many square mile area featuring thorny bushes, copious amounts of mud and extremely rutted roads. So, we decided to make some popcorn and watch some movies (Death of a Unicorn and Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows Part 2) rather than venturing out into the mud.

-Colin
campsites: Dispersed Camping North of Joshua Tree
